title: 10 Best Restaurants in Chiang Rai 2026: Khao Soi ฿40 & Night Bazaar Eats
meta_description: Khao soi from ฿40, northern sausage ฿25, kaeng hung lay near the Night Bazaar. Where Chiang Rai locals eat — not the tour-bus spots.
intro: Chiang Rai is northern Thailand's culinary heartland — the city where khao soi curry noodles were perfected, where sai ua (northern pork sausage) perfumes every market, and where the hill-tribe influence makes the food distinct from anything you'll find in Bangkok. The restaurants below are all real, named places that locals and experienced travellers return to. Prices reflect the city's position as one of Thailand's most affordable destinations: a proper sit-down meal rarely tops ฿120, and street food runs ฿30–60. Khao soi is non-negotiable as your first meal.

items.name: Khao Soi Phor Jai
items.story: Khao soi is Chiang Rai's signature dish — egg noodles in a coconut-curry broth topped with crispy fried noodles — and Khao Soi Phor Jai (ร้านพอใจ ข้าวซอยไก่) on Jet Yod Road is the address locals point to first. Open since the 1980s, this family-run shophouse serves a single-minded menu: khao soi gai (chicken) and sai ua (northern pork sausage). The broth is rounder and less sweet than the tourist-facing versions, with a visible ring of chilli oil on top. Arrive by 11:00 because the kitchen closes when the pots run dry — usually by 14:00.
items.why_locals_love_it: One of the most affordable and consistently authentic bowls in the city. Locals praise the house-made curry paste and the generous portion of chicken — a full thigh, not scraps. At ฿40–50 a bowl it has barely risen in price in a decade.
insider_tips: Order sai ua alongside your khao soi — the northern sausage (฿25) pairs perfectly with the curry broth.
Arrive before 12:00; the restaurant often sells out of chicken by early afternoon.
Ask for 'phet nit noi' (a little spicy) if you want the traditional heat level — the default is mild for tourists.
items.price_reality: Khao soi ฿40–50 per bowl. Sai ua (northern sausage) ฿25. One of the cheapest quality bowls in Chiang Rai.
items.location_details: 1023/2 Jet Yod Road (ถนนเจ็ดยอด), Chiang Rai. A 10-minute walk north of the Night Bazaar. Look for the red plastic stools and the queue of motorbike taxis.
items.personal_moment: A first-time visitor sitting next to a group of monks at the shared tables quickly realises this is not a tourist restaurant — the menu is handwritten in Thai, the staff will mime the options, and the bowl that arrives is the real thing.

items.name: Lung Eed Local Food
items.story: Lung Eed (ลุงอีด) is the kind of place that appears on zero tourist maps but on every local's shortlist. It operates from a simple shophouse near the old bus station, serving northern Thai rice plates at prices that feel anachronistic. The menu rotates daily around whatever is fresh at the morning market: kaeng hung lay (Burmese-style pork and ginger curry), laab khua (dry-fried minced pork with herbs), and nam prik ong (northern chilli relish with fried pork rinds). Point at whatever looks good in the trays — that's how regulars do it.
items.why_locals_love_it: Government office workers and market vendors eat here on rotation. The kaeng hung lay in particular — slow-cooked pork belly with tamarind and ginger — is as close to home cooking as you will find outside someone's house. A full plate of rice with two curries costs ฿50–70.
insider_tips: Come at 11:00–13:00 when the selection is fullest; many dishes sell out by 14:00.
The nam prik ong served with raw cabbage and pork rinds is a northern Thai staple worth trying even if it looks unfamiliar.
No English menu — point at the dishes in the trays or say the dish name. Staff are patient.
items.price_reality: Rice with two curries ฿50–70. Single curry side dish ฿20–30. One of the best value lunches in central Chiang Rai.
items.location_details: Near the old Chiang Rai bus terminal, Prasopsuk Road area. Ask locals for 'Lung Eed khao gaeng' — the name is well known in the neighbourhood.
items.personal_moment: Watching a construction crew pile in at noon, order without looking at any menu, and eat in twelve minutes flat is a reliable indicator that you have found a place that feeds the city rather than performs for it.

items.name: Cabbages & Condoms Chiang Rai
items.story: The Chiang Rai branch of the Population & Community Development Association's restaurant chain occupies a large, open compound on Thanalai Road. The name is deliberately provocative — the PDA uses shock value to destigmatise family planning in rural Thailand, and the dining room is decorated with contraceptive art installations. Behind the gimmick is genuinely good Thai food: tom kha gai, green curry, pad krapao, and a wide northern Thai selection. All profits fund community development and HIV/AIDS prevention programmes in the region's hill-tribe villages.
items.why_locals_love_it: The social mission gives Chiang Rai residents a pride-of-place connection to the restaurant. It is also one of the few places in the city that handles large groups without degrading food quality, making it a reliable choice for family dinners.
insider_tips: The complimentary condoms you receive instead of after-dinner mints are intentional — take them, it supports the cause.
Request a table in the garden section for the most atmospheric setting.
The set menus (฿250–350/person) offer the best value if you are dining with two or more people.
items.price_reality: Main dishes ฿120–220. Set menus ฿250–350 per person. Upscale by Chiang Rai standards but justified by the quality and the social mission.
items.location_details: 620/25 Thanalai Road (ถนนธนาลัย), Chiang Rai city centre. Open daily 10:00–24:00. Five minutes' walk from the Night Bazaar.
items.personal_moment: The first-time reaction when a diner realises that the decorative display on the wall is made entirely of condom wrappers — followed by the realisation that the tom kha is excellent — neatly captures what makes this restaurant memorable.

items.name: Phu-Lae Restaurant
items.story: Phu-Lae is one of the most established northern Thai restaurants in the city, operating for over two decades near the Night Bazaar on Phahonyothin Road. It occupies a traditional wooden Thai house with open-air sala seating in the garden. The menu is a comprehensive tour of Lanna (northern Thai) cuisine: kaeng hung lay, sai ua grilled at the table, khao niao (sticky rice), and jaew bong (roasted chilli paste). It is a reliable choice for groups who want the northern Thai experience with table service and an English menu.
items.why_locals_love_it: Phu-Lae has a reputation for consistency — the kaeng hung lay tastes the same whether you visit on a Tuesday or a Saturday. Locals bring visiting family here when they want to show off Chiang Rai food without having to explain what everything is.
insider_tips: Order the grilled sai ua at the table rather than the pre-cooked version — the char adds a layer of smokiness.
The jaew bong (roasted chilli and shrimp paste) served with sticky rice is free with most mains — ask for a refill.
Evenings are lively; arrive by 18:30 to avoid a wait on weekends.
items.price_reality: Main dishes ฿80–160. Full northern Thai spread for two people ฿300–400 including drinks. Mid-range for Chiang Rai.
items.location_details: Phahonyothin Road, near the Night Bazaar (ไนท์บาซาร์), Chiang Rai. The traditional wooden house structure is visible from the road.
items.personal_moment: The ritual of ordering sticky rice in a small wicker basket (kratib), pinching off a ball, and using it to scoop up curry is the defining tactile experience of northern Thai eating — Phu-Lae is where many visitors encounter it for the first time.

items.name: BaanChivitMai Bakery
items.story: BaanChivitMai (บ้านชีวิตใหม่, 'New Life Home') is a charity bakery run by the BaanChivitMai Foundation, which provides education, safe housing, and vocational training for hill-tribe children and young people who have been orphaned or are at risk of trafficking. The bakery trains beneficiaries in professional baking skills. The product — breads, pastries, cakes, sandwiches, and specialty coffee — is genuinely excellent, not charity-grade. The atmosphere is calm and cafe-like, popular with Chiang Rai's expat community and Thai NGO workers.
items.why_locals_love_it: Every purchase directly funds the foundation's work. The cinnamon rolls, banana bread, and locally sourced drip coffee have built a loyal following entirely on merit. There is a second BaanChivitMai cafe on the road to Doi Tung.
insider_tips: The foundation also operates a gift shop with hill-tribe handicrafts — a more meaningful souvenir than anything in the Night Bazaar.
Drip coffee uses beans from hill-tribe cooperative farms in the Chiang Rai mountains — ask the barista which farm is currently featured.
Visit the website (baanchivitmaikery.com) to read about the children the bakery supports if you want context for what your bill is funding.
items.price_reality: Coffee ฿60–90. Pastries and cakes ฿45–90. Sandwiches and light meals ฿80–130. Western-leaning prices but the social mission justifies every baht.
items.location_details: Main branch on Prasanmit Road, Chiang Rai city. A second branch is located on the road toward Doi Tung / Mae Sai. Check the foundation's website for current opening hours.
items.personal_moment: Reading the story cards on the wall about individual young people the foundation has helped while eating a cinnamon roll baked by one of their graduates puts the quality of the food in a different kind of context.

items.name: Chivit Thamma Da
items.story: Chivit Thamma Da (ชีวิตธรรมดา) sits on the north bank of the Mae Kok River, a 10-minute tuk-tuk ride from the city centre. The setting is exceptional: colonial-style wooden buildings in a riverside garden shaded by enormous trees, with views of the river and the hills beyond. The menu spans Thai and Western dishes — pad thai, green curry, salads, sandwiches, and excellent desserts. It doubles as a bookshop and event space. Chiang Rai's creative community — architects, artists, NGO workers — treats it as a second office.
items.why_locals_love_it: The riverside garden is one of the most beautiful dining settings in northern Thailand. On a cool-season morning (November to February) with a coffee and a book, it is genuinely hard to leave. Food quality is reliably good rather than exceptional.
insider_tips: The riverside tables book up quickly on weekend afternoons — arrive before 15:00 or after 19:00.
Cool-season evenings (November–February) are the best time to visit: temperatures drop to 15–18°C and the garden lights come on.
The bookshop section stocks an impressive range of English-language books on Thailand, Lanna history, and hill-tribe culture.
items.price_reality: Coffee and drinks ฿60–100. Light meals and desserts ฿80–160. Full Thai or Western mains ฿130–220. Slightly above average for Chiang Rai but the setting justifies it.
items.location_details: Keha Chiang Rai Road, on the north bank of the Mae Kok River. Approximately 2 km north of the city centre. Take a tuk-tuk (฿60–80) or cross the Mae Kok River footbridge on foot.
items.personal_moment: The sight of the morning mist burning off the Mae Kok River from a riverside table, coffee in hand, is a Chiang Rai moment that does not require a temple or a night market to feel complete.

items.name: Night Bazaar Food Court (Phahonyothin Road)
items.story: The Chiang Rai Night Bazaar on Phahonyothin Road has a covered food court at its centre that runs every evening from around 18:00 until midnight. Unlike Bangkok's tourist night markets, the food here is predominantly aimed at local diners: bowls of khao soi for ฿50, grilled corn, mango sticky rice, skewers of moo ping (grilled pork), and rotating regional specials. There is a permanent cluster of stalls at the back of the market that locals refer to as the 'real' food section — plastic tables on the ground, paper napkins, and menus in Thai only.
items.why_locals_love_it: It is where Chiang Rai's mixed population — Thai, Chinese-Thai, hill-tribe communities, students — all converge for cheap, fast dinner. The variety within 50 metres is hard to match at restaurant prices.
insider_tips: The stalls toward the back of the market (away from the souvenir section) have lower prices and serve a more local clientele.
Moo ping (grilled pork skewers) stalls set up from 17:30 — the first batch of the evening is always the best.
Bring small bills (฿20, ฿50) — most stalls do not carry much change for large notes.
items.price_reality: Street food ฿30–60 per item. A full dinner of multiple dishes costs ฿100–150. Among the best value evening meals in northern Thailand.
items.location_details: Phahonyothin Road (ถนนพหลโยธิน), central Chiang Rai. The Night Bazaar runs nightly from approximately 18:00. Unmissable — it is the largest evening market in the city centre.
items.personal_moment: Negotiating a khao soi bowl, a bag of fresh lychees, and a mango sticky rice dessert for under ฿150 total, then finding a plastic stool and watching the market build around you across a Thursday evening, is quintessential Chiang Rai.

items.name: Khun Churn Vegetarian Restaurant
items.story: Khun Churn (ขุนจร) is Chiang Rai's best-known vegetarian restaurant, operating a buffet-style format where dishes are displayed in trays and priced by weight. The menu draws on northern Thai cooking — curries, stir-fries, soups, and salads — reinterpreted without meat. Many dishes use mushrooms, tofu, and local vegetables grown in the highland areas north of the city. The buffet turns over frequently throughout the lunch service, so food is consistently fresh.
items.why_locals_love_it: Chiang Rai has a significant Buddhist community and a growing number of health-conscious younger residents. Khun Churn is where both groups eat without compromise on flavour. The price per gram model means you can eat as much or as little as you want.
insider_tips: The lunch buffet (11:00–14:00) is the main event — arrive around 11:30 for the widest selection.
The tam khanun (jackfruit salad) and kaeng liang (vegetable soup) are consistently among the best dishes in the trays.
A full plate costs ฿60–80 depending on what you select — one of the best value meals in the city.
items.price_reality: Buffet priced by weight: ฿60–90 for a full plate. Drinks extra. Lunch only (approximately 11:00–14:00 or until sold out).
items.location_details: Sankhongnoi Road area, Chiang Rai city. The restaurant is well known among locals — ask at your guesthouse or a motorbike taxi for current directions, as the exact street address is in Thai.
items.personal_moment: Being the only foreigner in a lunch crowd of Thai Buddhist families and local university students is a reliable sign that a vegetarian restaurant has got the food right — Khun Churn earns this consistently.

items.name: Nam Ngiao Pa Suk
items.story: Nam ngiao is the noodle soup that Chiang Rai does differently from anywhere else in Thailand: a tomato-based pork broth (distinct from both khao soi and standard Thai noodle soups) with pork ribs, blood cake, and dried spur chilli. It is the local answer to what foreigners expect 'northern Thai noodles' to be. Pa Suk on Sankhongnoi Road serves a focused menu of nam ngiao and is cited in Wikivoyage as one of the reliable addresses for the dish, with bowls at ฿35 — one of the lowest-priced sit-down dishes in the city.
items.why_locals_love_it: Nam ngiao is not widely known outside northern Thailand, which means restaurants that do it well command strong local loyalty. Pa Suk's broth — built on dried tomatoes, pork bones, and a paste of dried spur chilli — has a depth that takes hours to achieve.
insider_tips: Nam ngiao traditionally contains dok ngiao flowers (dried cotton tree blossoms) which contribute a slight bitterness — ask for it if you want the fully traditional version.
Order the 'special' (pho phi-set) with extra pork ribs for ฿45–50 — worth the upgrade.
The restaurant closes by 15:00 — this is a morning and lunch dish in northern Thai culture.
items.price_reality: Nam ngiao ฿35–50 per bowl. One of the cheapest proper sit-down meals in Chiang Rai.
items.location_details: 197 Sankhongnoi Road (ถนนสันโค้งน้อย), Chiang Rai. Open 09:00–15:00. A short ride from the city centre by songthaew or motorbike taxi.
items.personal_moment: Ordering a dish by pointing at someone else's bowl, receiving it, and discovering an entirely new soup architecture — tomato-forward, smoky, with a tangle of rice noodles and a pork rib — is the kind of accidental discovery that makes food travel worthwhile.

items.name: The Salad Concept
items.story: The Salad Concept is a Thai-owned modern cafe-restaurant that opened in Chiang Rai as part of a small chain. It occupies a clean, air-conditioned space and serves build-your-own salads, sandwiches, pasta, and smoothie bowls alongside Thai dishes. It is not a traditional northern Thai experience, but it fills a gap: Chiang Rai has a growing population of Thai university students, digital nomads, and long-stay visitors who want a reliable wifi-friendly lunch that is not pad thai. The ingredients are fresher and more varied than most comparable cafes.
items.why_locals_love_it: Younger Chiang Rai residents — students from Rajabhat University, staff from the hospital, NGO workers — treat it as a weekday lunch staple. The portion-to-price ratio is fair, the wifi works, and the air conditioning is reliable in the hot season (March–May).
insider_tips: The 'create your own salad' bowls with quinoa and imported greens run ฿120–160 — high by Chiang Rai street standards but reasonable for the ingredients.
The fresh-pressed juices (฿60–80) using local tropical fruit are better than the smoothie bowls.
Busiest at 12:00–13:30 on weekdays — arrive before noon or after 13:30 for faster service.
items.price_reality: Salads and light meals ฿90–160. Smoothies and juices ฿60–80. Mid-range for Chiang Rai; notably more expensive than street food but the ingredient quality reflects it.
items.location_details: Central Chiang Rai, near the Clock Tower (นาฬิกาเมืองเชียงราย) area. Multiple locations in the city — check current branch addresses locally as the chain has expanded.
items.personal_moment: Sitting with a bowl of actual greens after four days of rice and curry is not a defeat — it is a recalibration that makes the next bowl of khao soi taste even better.

local_wisdom: Khao soi in Chiang Rai is made with egg noodles in a coconut-curry broth — different from the clear-broth versions served elsewhere. If a restaurant gives you clear broth, you are in the wrong place.
The cool season (November–February) is when Chiang Rai's outdoor dining is at its best: temperatures drop to 10–15°C at night and every garden restaurant fills with locals in fleece jackets.

Northern Thai food uses more dried spices, fermented soybean (tua nao), and bitter vegetables than central Thai cuisine. It is less sweet, more herbaceous, and often served with sticky rice rather than jasmine rice.
budget_hacks: The Night Bazaar food court on Phahonyothin Road is the single best place to eat well for under ฿150 total — multiple dishes, real local food, no tourist markup.
Lunch is cheaper than dinner at most Chiang Rai restaurants: the same khao gaeng (rice and curry) plate that costs ฿60 at lunch may be ฿90 at dinner.
Songthaews (shared red trucks) cost ฿20–30 per trip within the city — far cheaper than tuk-tuks for reaching restaurants outside the centre like Chivit Thamma Da.
cultural_notes: Northern Thai food (Lanna cuisine) is a distinct culinary tradition from central Thai cooking. Key dishes — khao soi, nam ngiao, sai ua, kaeng hung lay — are specific to Chiang Rai, Chiang Mai, and the surrounding highland provinces. When eating khao soi, the correct method is to mix the crispy noodles on top into the broth as you eat, squeeze in the lime wedge, and add the pickled mustard greens from the small side dish. Sticky rice is pinched off the ball with the right hand and used to scoop up curry — not eaten with chopsticks or a spoon.
perplexity_query: Top 10 best restaurants in Chiang Rai, Thailand in 2025. Include for each: current prices in Thai Baht, opening hours, exact location/address, recent reviews or ratings, specialties/signature dishes, any new openings in 2024-2025. Focus on authentic local favorites and highly rated establishments.
